question_text: A winemaker in a northern European region has just finished a growing season marked by cold, rainy weather. [2] Chaptalisation process is not intended to make the wine sweeter, but rather to provide more sugar for the yeast to ferment into alcohol.
---
[3] Chaptalisation if the must has excess sugar for normal fermentation, water may be added to lower the concentration.

options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'Use the controlled-origin wine certification for both items, since other European countries modeled all food and wine place-name rules directly on France, and that status travels unchanged abroad.'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': "Use Switzerland's controlled-origin wine certification for the wine, use the protected-origin designation for the non-wine product, and recognize that the wine's place-name protection might not be fully enforceable in overseas markets."}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Use the protected-origin designation for both the wine and the non-wine product, because Switzerland applies one origin scheme across all estate goods, and foreign recognition is automatic.'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Avoid any Swiss origin certification for the wine because only food products qualify, but apply the wine certification to the non-wine product and expect stronger protection outside Europe.'}]
source_facts:
[1] === Switzerland ===

Switzerland has an appellation d'origine contrôlée certification for wines and an appellation d'origine protégée certification for other food products.
---
[2] === Other EU countries ===
Many other countries have based their controlled place name systems on the French AOC classification.
---
[3] == International trade issues ==
The protections afforded by the AOC status may not be available in foreign markets.
